questions.question: Without any index, how does a database find rows matching `WHERE email = 'x'`?
options: It instantly knows the location of every value
It performs a full table scan, checking every row — O(n)
It refuses to run the query
It asks the operating system to sort the table first
questions.explanation: A table is an unordered heap by default, so without an index the database must examine every row to find matches — a full table scan. This is O(n) and fine for small tables but devastating as the table grows to millions of rows.

questions.question: Are indexes free to add?
options: Yes — add one to every column for maximum speed
No — each index must be kept in sync, so every insert/update/delete does more work and uses more disk
Yes — indexes only cost a tiny amount of memory once
No — but only because they require a license
questions.explanation: Every index is extra sorted data the database maintains. Each write must update the table and every relevant index, so over-indexing a write-heavy table slows writes and bloats disk. You index for important queries, not everything.

questions.question: In `EXPLAIN QUERY PLAN` output, what does seeing `SEARCH users USING INDEX ...` instead of `SCAN users` tell you?
options: The query failed
The database is using an index to jump to matching rows rather than scanning the whole table
The table has no rows
The index is corrupt
questions.explanation: `SCAN` means a full table scan (no usable index); `SEARCH ... USING INDEX` means the database is using the index for an O(log n) lookup. Flipping a query from SCAN to SEARCH by adding the right index is the core skill of indexing.

questions.question: You have a composite index on `(city, created)`. Which query can it help, per the leftmost-prefix rule?
options: WHERE created = '2024-01-15' (filtering only on created)
WHERE city = 'city42' (filtering on the leftmost column)
Any query on the table, regardless of columns
Only queries that use neither column
questions.explanation: A composite index is sorted by its columns left to right. It serves queries filtering a leftmost prefix — so `city` alone, or `city` + `created`, work; but `created` alone skips the leading column and cannot use the index.

questions.question: Why might a database IGNORE an index and choose a full scan anyway?
options: Indexes never actually work
When the query matches most of the rows, scanning can be cheaper than millions of individual index lookups
Because scans are always faster than indexes
Because EXPLAIN disables all indexes
questions.explanation: The query planner estimates cost. If a query matches a large fraction of rows (say 90%), doing an index lookup per match is more work than just scanning sequentially, so the planner correctly chooses a scan. Always verify with the query plan rather than assuming.
